core_intuitions: One request fans out into three complementary signals — traces (causal per-request chain), metrics (aggregated time-series), logs (verbatim prompt/response text) — and no single pillar is sufficient on its own.

Evaluation in production runs OFF the critical path: the user's response is returned immediately, while a strategically-sampled copy of traffic (100% of failures, higher for new prompts, ~5% baseline) is judged asynchronously so eval never adds request latency.

Three distinct kinds of drift each need a different detector: input/covariate shift (PSI comparing a current prompt distribution to a reference), output-quality drift (CUSUM accumulating small sustained score drops until a threshold), and provider/model-version drift (an hourly canary catching a silent model swap behind the same API).
The LLMOps lifecycle is a closed loop — Ship a canary, Observe, Evaluate a sample, Improve, Retrain — where production logs feed the data flywheel that makes the next version better.

Burn-rate alerting (integrating an error budget over a rolling window) beats fixed thresholds: it ignores brief noisy spikes that cause alarm fatigue while catching genuine sustained degradation faster.

existing.name: obs-three-pillars-pipeline
existing.improvement: Correctly and clearly conveys the fan-out of one request into trace/metric/log stores; reads well in both light and dark themes with legible labels, coherent color-coded arrows, and no clipping. No change needed.

existing.name: obs-llmops-lifecycle-loop
existing.issue: In dark theme the five node labels use dark --ink-soft text over the translucent accent-soft purple fill, which sits on the dark page background and reads muddy / low-contrast (visibly weaker than the crisp light text in the three-pillars figure). The circular arrows also convey direction but the loop-closure intent (5 -> back to 1) is only implied.
existing.improvement: Make node text theme-aware so it is a light high-contrast ink in dark mode (use --ink rather than --ink-soft, or back each node with a more opaque --surface layer beneath the translucent accent-soft fill so the label always meets contrast). Verify the number badges do not overlap the box corner text. Optionally emphasize that phase 5 -> 1 closes the loop (e.g. a subtle 'flywheel' full-circle cue) so the continuous nature is unmistakable.

new_figures.name: obs-eval-in-production-async-sampling
new_figures.concept: How production eval stays off the request critical path: response returns immediately while a strategically-sampled copy is judged asynchronously.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single most load-bearing operational idea in the chapter and it is currently unillustrated. Readers must grok TWO things at once: (1) the async split — the user never waits for the judge — and (2) strategic sampling — you cannot judge everything, so a policy gate decides what gets evaluated. Text alone makes this easy to conflate with synchronous guardrails; a picture separates the fast synchronous lane from the slow asynchronous evaluation lane and makes the sampling funnel concrete.
new_figures.placement: Under the '## Evaluation in Production' heading, right after the intro paragraph and before '### Sampling strategy'.
new_figures.brief: A two-lane data-flow infographic that visually separates a fast synchronous lane from a slow asynchronous lane. TOP LANE (bold, solid arrows, labeled 'critical path — synchronous'): User -> LLM Service -> Response, drawn as a straight left-to-right flow that loops the response straight back to the User; annotate this lane 'returns immediately — never waits for eval'. From the LLM Service, draw a THIN branch that drops a COPY of each completed request downward into a SAMPLING GATE (render as a decision/funnel element): show the policy rows as small labeled bars, wide-to-narrow, using the chapter's illustrative example rates — '100% of failures / safety-flagged', '~30% of new prompt-template traffic', '~50% of previously low-scoring', '~5% baseline random'; a wide faint arrow labeled 'not sampled — dropped' exits the gate to the side to show most traffic is NOT judged. The narrow 'sampled' output feeds an ASYNC EVAL QUEUE (draw as a small buffer of stacked items). Enclose the queue + judge in a dashed boundary labeled 'asynchronous — separate process / worker pool (off the critical path)'. Inside: a JUDGE (LLM-as-judge) box scoring three axes -> composite, e.g. small chips 'faithfulness / relevance / fluency -> composite'. The composite scores flow back OUT of the async boundary via a return arrow that writes to the LOG / TRACE store 'attached by trace ID', which in turn feeds 'dashboards + data flywheel'. The key visual contrast to make unmistakable: the synchronous response arrow is short and bold; the eval path is a long dashed detour that rejoins only the log store, never the user. Theme-safe: use the accent color for the sampled/eval path and a muted neutral for the dropped path; only the small illustrative percentages above (which come from the chapter's own example policy) as numbers — invent no latency or throughput figures. Everything must fit inside the viewBox with clear top-to-bottom then left reading order and no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: obs-drift-detection-triptych
new_figures.concept: The three kinds of drift and the distinct detector each requires: PSI on input distributions, CUSUM on output quality, hourly canary on provider version.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter presents three drift types with dense math (PSI sum, CUSUM recurrence) but no visual. The intuition behind each detector is inherently graphical and hard to extract from formulas: PSI is 'two histograms pulling apart', CUSUM is 'small persistent dips accumulating past a threshold that a single-point alarm would miss', and provider drift is 'a flat canary that steps down when the vendor silently swaps the model'. A shared-theme triptych makes all three click and cements that drift is not one thing but three, each monitored differently.
new_figures.placement: Immediately under the '## Drift Detection' heading, before '### Input drift (covariate shift)'.
new_figures.brief: Three side-by-side panels sharing a common 'reference vs now' visual theme, each with a small title, a schematic plot, and a one-line takeaway. PANEL 1 — 'Input drift (covariate shift) -> PSI': two overlaid binned distributions over the same x-axis (label it generically, e.g. 'prompt embedding PC1' or 'prompt token count'): a muted/neutral REFERENCE histogram and an accent-colored CURRENT histogram that is visibly shifted and reshaped relative to it; add a small interpretation scale bar 'PSI < 0.1 stable | 0.1-0.25 moderate | > 0.25 significant' with a marker sitting in the 'significant' zone; takeaway: 'prompts changed — earlier eval scores no longer representative'. PANEL 2 — 'Output-quality drift -> CUSUM': a time axis with a rolling composite eval-score line that wobbles around an in-control mean (draw a faint horizontal mean line), then undergoes a small SUSTAINED downward step partway through; BELOW or overlaid on a second baseline, draw the CUSUM S_n curve that stays flat near zero during the noisy period and then ramps steadily upward once the true mean drops, crossing a dashed decision threshold h where an 'alert' flag fires; takeaway: 'accumulates small persistent drops a single-point threshold would miss'. PANEL 3 — 'Provider / model-version drift -> hourly canary': a fixed probe-set judge score plotted at regular hourly ticks (show a little clock or 'hourly' cadence cue) sitting flat, then a STEP DROP at one tick with an annotation 'same API label, model snapshot silently changed' and an 'alert' flag; takeaway: 'pin versions; a canary catches silent swaps'. Keep all curves schematic — generic axis labels ('score', 'time'), NO invented benchmark numbers; the only textual thresholds are the PSI interpretation bands quoted from the chapter. Theme-safe colors (muted for reference/baseline, accent for the drifted/alerting element, a distinct warning-neutral for the alert flags). Panels must be equal-width, fit inside the viewBox, and each be independently legible with no cross-panel overlap.

new_figures.name: obs-burn-rate-vs-fixed-threshold
new_figures.concept: Why burn-rate alerting reduces false positives while catching real degradation faster than a fixed threshold.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's practitioner tip claims burn-rate alerting 'dramatically reduces false positives while catching genuine degradations faster', but the reason is only graspable visually: a noisy signal crosses a fixed line many times (alarm fatigue), whereas an integrated error-budget burn barely reacts to brief spikes yet ramps fast on a sustained problem. This is a genuine 'aha' that a single contrasting picture delivers far better than prose, and it generalizes the SRE error-budget model the chapter leans on.
new_figures.placement: Next to the practitioner-tip callout 'use composite burn-rate alerts, not fixed thresholds' in the '## What to Monitor and Alert On' section.
new_figures.brief: Two vertically stacked timelines of the SAME noisy quality/error signal, sharing one x-axis (time). TOP — 'Fixed threshold': the jittery signal with several brief upward spikes that momentarily cross a horizontal dashed threshold line, plus one region of GENUINE sustained degradation; mark EVERY threshold crossing with an alert flag, so the brief spikes produce a cluster of flags labeled collectively 'false alarms -> alarm fatigue', and the sustained region also flags. Visually communicate 'too many alerts, mostly noise'. BOTTOM — 'Burn rate (error-budget)': the same underlying signal transformed into a rolling error-budget-consumption curve over a window; the brief spikes barely nudge the burn curve (stays well under the line -> NO alert), while during the sustained degradation the burn curve climbs quickly and crosses a line labeled 'burn rate > 2x budget' -> a SINGLE correctly-timed alert flag, annotated 'fires faster on the real problem, ignores noise'. Use only small illustrative annotations from the chapter ('1% error budget', '2x budget rate'); invent no other numbers. Theme-safe: neutral/muted for the raw signal, accent for the burn curve and the correct alert, a warning-neutral for the false-alarm cluster. Both timelines must align on the x-axis, fit inside the viewBox, and read top (naive) to bottom (better) with legible labels and no overlap.

summary: The chapter is concept-dense but figure-poor: only two figures cover a long LLMOps chapter. Both existing figures are strong — keep the three-pillars pipeline as-is; refine the lifecycle loop for dark-mode label contrast. The two most foundational under-served intuitions each warrant a crisp static infographic: (1) eval-in-production running asynchronously off the critical path with a strategic sampling gate, and (2) the three-kinds-of-drift triptych (PSI / CUSUM / hourly canary) that turns the chapter's dense drift math into visual intuition. A third, lower-priority figure contrasts burn-rate alerting against fixed thresholds to land the chapter's SRE error-budget tip. All three are fully understandable as static frames, theme-safe, and use only the chapter's own illustrative integers.
